sentences.text: Yes, they passed these.
Then in the fall, I went to-- that time was junior high school, it was the last year of junior high school, the ninth grade.
I had taken those two subjects over again, because I know I-- I didn't go anywhere.
And I struggled, because it was difficult to find help to help me-- you know like now you have tutors.
And-- so I-- it was one of the toughest times, that year.
I mean, you hear all the kids, and you can't communicate.
And I felt probably a little different.
I was older.
But after the first year, things start getting better.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I went to summer school one year, day and night, so I could get out of high school sooner.
And while I was my senior year, I was-- I was already drafted in the Army.
And I was supposed to graduate in June, and they wanted me to go in the Army in-- in March.
It was in 1951-- the Korean War started.
Yes, the United States Army.
So--
No, you're not-- once you're a resident, you-- you know, you're eligible for the draft.
Once you become a permanent resident.
I was-- citizenship takes five years-- to get citizenship.
So I had to go in front of the board and-- and plead that I-- let me finish high school-- I mean, I have a few months.
And they let me do that.
And I graduated in June, July within the service.
[SIGHS] I'm glad you mentioned that, because my uncle was in the wholesale produce.
And he would get up, 2:00 in the morning, and go to work.
And he would get me up, 2:00 in the morning, and go to work.
And-- and then 7 o'clock, 7:30, I had to clean up and go to school-- I walked from their place, which was on-- not too-- to the bus, and-- and then go to school.
After school, I came back and helped unload trucks-- produce.
So this was not easy for me, the-- the-- the few years I had to-- 2:30 in the morning, and 3 o'clock in the morning, every day.
Seven, well, six days a week.
Sunday was-- six days a week.
And it was getting so that I looked forward to going in the Army.
I figured it can't be much worse.
[LAUGHS] I mean, it was really tough for me because I had to do homework.
And I would-- and I would-- after dinner, he would go to sleep, I would have to study.
I mean, prepare for the next day, do my homework, which was very hard for me anyway.
[SIGHS]
So it was tough.
I don't know what he had in mind-- he was a good person.
I mean, a good-natured person who would do things.
But maybe he wanted to toughen me up-- I mean-- like I didn't have-- had it easy before.
It was very, very tough.
I talk about it now, I didn't then-- I-- I kind of-- but I was-- it was-- bothered me a great deal.
Did you sell produce all morning?
What did you do?
No, I-- I helped load trucks, pack Brussels sprouts-- you know, and-- you know, the things-- there was always something to do.
And they-- and if you seen these little quart baskets of Brussels sprouts, I used to do that.
I used to pack about 300 before I went to school.
We used to deliver them to the chain stores, Kroger's, and Big Bear, and all these.
I was hard working, and I guess [LAUGHS] he liked that part of me.
But it was very, very difficult to be able to-- school and work.
So I did that till I went in the Army.
I was in the motorized infantry.
First, I was-- took basic training in-- in Pennsylvania.
And-- and the Korean War was all-- was on-- a lot of Americans being killed because of lack of training.
So they increased the training from eight weeks to 16 weeks.
And I thought that's where we're going to end up is Korea.
Fortunately, half the company they sent to Germany, and I was one of them.
So I went to Germany.
I left '48, came back in '51.
That didn't bother me.
I would have rather have gone to Germany than-- than Korea, because I-- to me, Korea didn't make sense.
Somewhere, nowhere, to fight for people you don't even know about, and you don't know if it's right or wrong.
In Germany, I felt different.
I came back as an American soldier, was proud, and, you know, was-- I felt a lot different about that.
And--
Well, it was not a great deal different from the time I was there, '48.
You-- you could see Munich was still destroyed, and other places.
I was in-- in the city of Ulm.
It was not-- didn't suffer anything, it's on the Danube-- new Ulm and old Ulm.
I was there for about six months, then we went to Nuremberg.
So we were transferred to Nuremberg.
And I constantly in the field, soldiered all the time.
But I was never-- but I had fun.
I made the best of it.
I mean-- we really-- it was tough, soldiering.
But, you know, among young people, and I had a good time when I was in the Army.
I didn't have it easy, but I guess I was used to that, but I had a good time-- I had good times.
I could speak-- I learned German fluent in a short time.
And everybody wanted to go with me downtown, or whatever.
So.
I-- I-- I never felt uncomfortable.
There was a-- strange that you brought it up, because I was jogging with two of my friends-- one is not Jewish-- and he said-- he talked something about the Army.
He says, how was it in the Army?
I said, you know there was one time.
It was this guy from Pittsburgh, and-- [?
Kissel, ?] and-- and he-- he would-- when I would go on a three-day pass on a holiday, I would come back.
He says-- and he would make, you know, remarks like, how was your rabbi?
You know, and things.
It aggravated me.
It bothered me.
And he was the kind of guy that nobody liked anyway.
I mean, I-- I-- you know, other than him, we didn't have-- didn't have any problem.
But one time, he got me so upset that I fought him.
And he was about a head taller than I am, but I beat him up.
And there were like 24 guys in that barrack, and they applauded me.
From then on, he became-- he was one of my friends.
But sometime I think how you gain respect is by fighting back.
But other than that, I never had any problems.
The Germans, they thought I was German.
So there was the big thing, and they would talk about things until the point I got-- and I told them, when it was convenient for me to upset them, then I said, I'm not German.
I'm a Jew.
That upset them.
Because they thought, oh, Miller, and, you know, maybe they thought I looked German, too.
So I spoke German.
But I-- it's strange-- there was not-- there was no hatred.
I mean, I despised some of the things.
I mean.
I just didn't carry that hatred with me.
In a way, I-- I was glad, because of some-- but I also didn't have great respect for them, for another, you know?
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
But considering the alternative to be in Korea, I was happy to be in Germany.
And things looked bad then, because there could have been a war with the Russians at any time.
The relations were not good, the Berlin blockade, and all kinds of things went on.
And we were constantly preparing for war.
I probably thought we wouldn't have a [?
China's ?] chance if there was a war in-- in Germany, because the Russians were so huge, with their land armies.
And they could overrun that part of Europe anyway.
But I hoped there would-- wouldn't be a war.
I-- I became corporal, and then they froze rank.
So I-- I-- I was a corporal.
I was a gunner-- was-- and the machine-- machine gun was my thing.
I mean, you, as an infantry man.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were constantly on maneuvers, constantly in the field.
You come in to clean up and take a break and back in the field.
So we were constantly on training, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] preparing.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
The-- well, the war, yeah, in 1953-- when I was-- yeah, in June 1953-- I-- I was-- my time-- my Army time was up, the two years were up.
And I came back to Columbus.
And at that time, I didn't know what to do.
I-- I did-- I went to Ohio State with a friend that made-- made sure that I registered for school.
And I was going to start school.
And I came back.
I was registered, and I was happy about that.
And I tell my uncle, guess what?
I just registered at Ohio State.
And he said, eh.
He says, you don't want to go to school.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I thought to myself, god, that-- I didn't expect that from him.
Because I-- what he meant to say, eh, you know, I should be working for him.
He didn't say it, but that's what he wanted me to do.
So I was terribly upset.
I was so upset, because I-- I thought he would say, god, I'm happy.
I'll help you, whatever, you know, all that.
Well, I didn't hear that.
So I went to Detroit.
I had this friend, Bakalar-- you remember that name, Bakalar, who was gonna go to Italy?
I found out he came to Detroit, and-- and I went to see him.
And he tried to-- he had a place for me to go to work.
I mean, you know, he had it all lined up.

And he really wanted me to come to-- to Detroit, because I think he had a-- he cared about me, and even though, you know, I was already out of the service and all that, he had a daughter, two, three years old, and she looked at me like an older brother.
[SIGHS] And I was all set to go.
But I had this guilt about my aunt-- you know, I lived with my aunt and uncle.
I didn't want to work for him, because I didn't want to get up at 2:00, 3:00 in the morning.
That was the biggest part-- if it was some decent hours, 50 hours a week, it would have been OK.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My uncle didn't communicate.
He didn't say, [INAUDIBLE] I want you to work for me, do this, and all that.
He didn't.
When he said that, I-- I got the idea that he doesn't want me to go to school.
And he-- you know-- so I didn't.
And before I went to Detroit, I felt guilty about my aunt.
You know, she-- I was gonna move out, and she would feel bad, like something-- you know, like-- they did the wrong things.
So I got a job at Lazarus.
I applied, actually applied about two, three weeks before that because a friend of mine worked there.
And I didn't hear from Lazarus-- Lazarus department store?
Lazarus department store.
So I went back, and I said, you know, two weeks ago I was here, and I made an application, and-- and I didn't hear from you.
I just want to know, can you let me know right now, whether I have a job or not?
Otherwise I'm going to Detroit.
And that's exactly how that was.
And right away, they-- they started working on it, and said, yeah, you can work in the shoe department.
So I didn't-- I wanted something to be away-- not get stuck at my uncle's place, because I didn't like that business.
Not so much liking the business-- that would have been OK.
It's the hours, the work.
So I started working at Lazarus.
So I did start at night, and I went at night for about a-- for over a year.
And then I had to work the nights, and I couldn't go.
But I was going off and on, whenever.
Yeah.
Oh, in 1974.
Yeah, in 1974.
Other than that, I didn't know anything about my brothers.
And I had no contacts, I had no addresses.
And in 1974, there was a letter that came to the Jewish Family Service.
And a cousin of mine and was over there, second cousin, he was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Herbert Wolman.
And-- in-- on that letter it says Wolman [?
Gehlen, ?] oh, Miller.
I mean, they had three names.
So he said-- he told somebody, says, contact Irv Miller.
And there was a letter of one of my brothers that-- in Russian, of course, I couldn't read it.
Somebody read it, and I immediately-- I forced myself to write in Yiddish-- I mean, I haven't had Yiddish in all these years.
And as a kid, I used to write Yiddish, when I was about-- was eight years old, I think.
And-- and I wrote him a letter.
And that's how we started communicating, in '74. '75, I went to Russia, I took a trip to Russia.
Yeah.
Well, yes, I was working at Lazarus.
And I went-- I was going to night school.
And-- and I-- that was a temporary job.
I wasn't going to stay long.
That's what I was thinking.
And I moved in with another roommate.
We got an apartment.
And my aunt was a lot like-- I mean, she treated me in a way she treated her children.
I mean, you know-- I mean, I felt like I'm part of the family.
But the kids, I felt I'm in their way, too-- their three children.
And I wanted to move out.
So-- but I never wanted to hurt her feelings.
So I would come two, three times a week at first, have dinner with them.
And she was comfortable with that.
And then on Friday nights, once a week.
So I had a great relationship with my aunt, although we used to argue at times.
But she felt comfortable with me.

I mean, she once said to me, she-- when she gave me a-- a nice present for when I got married, she says, I know, she says, if I have-- I had a problem I could always come to live with you, or something like that.
When she was so-- she said it, I felt so good about that, because like she really knew me, and I knew her.
So there was a good thing.
Where was I?
You thought you were going to be working temporarily at Lazarus.
Yeah, so with Lazarus, I just wanted a job so I wouldn't have to work with-- at my uncle's place.
I just couldn't take those hours-- they were just-- it was killing him, and-- and it was too rough.
I figured there's no money worth all that.
In fact, my uncle only lived till the age of 57.
He died at the age of 57.
He died of a heart attack.
And I'm sure the job had a-- has a lot to do with that.
He worked 18 hours a day, and the stress and all that was very tough.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Yes, I stayed at Lazarus.
I mean, I was in the department, I was in the carpet department after that.
And, you know, things were OK.
And-- so I wasn't looking for anything.
I was, yeah, I had-- yeah, I had-- I had a roommate.
We had a nice apartment.
Then I moved in-- I moved in by myself to an apartment, and I had a nice apartment.
And I worked there.
And then in 1968, I got married.
In '68-- in-- excuse me, '67.
Jonathan, we got Jonathan.
We adopted Jonathan in '68, and then--
'67, September, yeah, '67.
Yeah, she's-- she's from Toledo.
And we were married for about 15, 16 years.
And adopted my son, and we adopted a daughter, too-- Johanna.
She was adopted in '71.
Yeah, I talk to people, yes.
I talked to-- just bits and pieces.
In fact, I wanted to talk to my children, tell them more-- but they ask questions, I talk to them about it.
But I-- I always wanted them to ask me rather than me telling them.
I-- I just didn't want to force it.
I want them to be interested, and I never felt that they were really interested.
Although Jonathan has very strange ways about saying that-- he would say, Dad, I heard you tell this.
But he always listened in when I talked to someone else.
But on a one-to-one, I always expect my son to say, hey, Dad, tell me what really happened.
And that never happened.
A friend of mine who survived with his family used to say, I tell it to my kids whether they want to hear it or not-- [?
Herv ?] [?
Shamas. ?] You know.
And I thought, I don't-- I don't buy that.
Well, yes, people ask me about things.
In fact, when I-- we trained for the marathon, so I had so many friends.
And so many friends, some things come out.
And I would talk about it.
And then I-- I didn't feel uncomfortable, but just, you know, bits and pieces.
And it seems that people, they wanted to hear it.
I didn't want to-- too much of it, but--
We were running for a marathon.
We had-- so I was always with a group of people.
And there's a couple fellas always wanted to know.
They asked me things.
And they-- they always seemed to want to hear it.
So I talked to them about it.
I took up running in '68, '68.
I actually was one of oldest runners at the Jewish Center.
In fact, they had a trophy with my name, Irv Miller's 10,000 Miles-- that's-- that's 20,000 miles before that or longer.
So I was-- been doing this for years.
Yeah, we talked-- yeah, bits and pieces.
But never-- it-- yeah, I think we did.
But not that much-- not that much.
[INAUDIBLE].
With my cousin, I talked more about it.
But-- but they never heard the whole story, never did.
Yeah, I talked to her about it.
But never, you know, never like this.
Never went through all that.
That's why I used to-- I wanted Jonathan to-- to sit in for once to hear me talk about it-- he would hear the whole story, rather than-- or most of the whole story, rather than bits and pieces.
And-- although he would say, I heard that, I heard that.
I'm sure during the years, things-- bits and pieces came out.
It's just, for-- for all these years, I thought of writing my story.
I thought it-- it was unique.
[SIGHS] I used to think, if somebody told me my story, I would never believe him, because I thought it was almost impossible to go through what I did.
And when I tell it, it doesn't seem like it was really that hard.
I mean, when I talk about it.
But to live through, minute by minute, and day by day, and hour-- you know, it just seemed endless.
And-- I mean to-- to be afraid of everything-- I mean, it's like you have no friends.
You're totally alone, totally alone.
I mean, the only people who were good, you come in for awhile, and they give you some food, and they like for you to leave, not to stay there.
I mean, just think, for three years, I've never taken a bath.
I never changed clothes-- I mean, unless they disappeared.
They just wore out.
So it was tough.
But then you hear about these concentration camp people, and they had it like-- they lived through something that is unbearable.
It's also, you know-- I'm not trying to compare which is worse, or which is better, but it was tough.
So now, when I tell this story, it's-- it's quite different from the way I used to think.
Well, let's stop here.
